2: In order to inform the identification of appropriate strategies towards envisioned futures for food production, it is instructive to consider the past. For example, to what extent have changing technologies influenced the sustainability profile of food produc- tion over time? Have conditions generally improved, or are we producing food in ways that are less sustainable than those that were commonplace decades or centuries ago? What key factors have influenced changes in the sustainability profile of food

systems, and what lessons might these changes provide in support of formulating paths forward with respect to sustainable intensi- fication objectives (WRI, 2013; Godfray and Garnett, 2014; Rockstrom et al., 2017)? Agricultural production norms have evolved continuously over time (Hamilton et al., 2013; Yang and Suh, 2015). In particular, the emergence of new technologies and improved management practices has enabled more efficient use of finite resources such as land, water and energy (WRI, 2013; Pelletier et al., 2014; Yang and Suh, 2015).

Temporally-resolved studies of changes in the resource and environmental footprints of several livestock sectors have been reported in recent years (Cederberg et al., 2009; Vergé et al., 2009; Capper et al., 2009; Capper, 2011; Boyd and Cady, 2012; Hamilton

et al., 2013; Pelletier et al., 2014). For example, Capper (2011) re- ported substantial gains in resource use efficiency (69.9% of ani- mals, 81.4% of feedstuffs, 87.9% of the water, and 67.0% of the land required) per kilogram of beef produced in the US, along with a 16.3% reduction in GHG emissions, between 1977 and 2007. For dairy in 2007 compared to 1944, milk production (per kg) was reported to require 21% of the animals, 23% of feedstuffs, 35% of the water, and only 10% of the land, while emitting only 37% of GHGs (Capper et al., 2009). Vergé et al. (2009) found that the GHG in- tensity of egg production decreased from 1.9 kg of CO2 equivalent emissions/dozen eggs in 1981 to 1.76 kg of CO2 equivalent emis- sions/dozen eggs in 2006, an approximately 7% reduction over the 25-yr interval.

Life cycle thinking and related tools such as life cycle assessment (LCA) have been central to our evolving understanding of the cur- rent distribution of resource use, emissions, and sustainability impacts that are characteristic of contemporary food product sup- ply chains (Pelletier, 2015; Castellani et al., 2017; Notarnicola et al., 2017). Rigorously answering questions regarding changes in the sustainability impacts of food production over time similarly re- quires a life cycle approach that takes into account shifts in prac- tices and production efficiencies along the entirety of the supply chains that ultimately support food production (Pelletier et al., 2014). A variety of authors have used LCA to study egg production systems in different contexts (Mollenhorst et al., 2006; Cederberg et al., 2009; Vergé et al., 2009; Wiedemann and McGahan, 2011; Leinonen et al., 2012; Pelletier et al., 2013, 2014, 2017; Ghasempour and Ahmadi, 2016). However, among those seeking to understand evolution in the environmental impacts associated with the production of eggs or other livestock products over time, changes in the efficiencies and impacts characteristic of the "up- stream" processes that ultimately provide inputs to livestock pro- duction have typically not been considered.

Building on a recent benchmarking study of life cycle impacts for Canadian egg products (Pelletier, 2017) and methods for temporally-resolved agri-food supply chain modeling developed by Pelletier et al. (2014), a comprehensive assessment of the resource and environmental implications of changes in conventional Cana- dian egg production supply chains over a 50 year interval spanning 1962-2012 was undertaken. This included characterization of farm-level changes, as well as changes in important supply chain activities including the efficiency of fossil energy and electricity production, fertilizer production, feed input production, and freight distribution. These changes were evaluated in terms of their impact on cradle-to-farm gate water, land and energy use, along with greenhouse gas, acidifying, and eutrophying emissions - both per unit of eggs produced, and for industry-wide production in aggregate. On this basis, the life cycle resource and environmental performance gains associated with specific technological and husbandry advancements over the past 50 years were identified, differentiating between changes attributable to supply chain versus farm-level activities.

6: 2.1. Goal and scope of the study

The general objective of this study was to perform a cradle-to- farm gate environmental life cycle assessment (i.e. environmental footprint study) of conventional Canadian egg production in 1962 compared to 2012 based on the methodological specifications of ISO 14044 (ISO, 2006). The specific objectives were to:
1) Characterize average egg production supply chain conditions in 2012 and 1962, including:

<list>a Pullet and layer house-specific variables such as feed con- version efficiency, feed composition, bird body weight, bird mortality rate, egg production per hen housed, water usage, and manure handling 
b Geographically and temporally-resolved efficiencies in important supply activities including the provision of primary energy carriers (i.e. oil, gas, coal, etc.) and electricity, freight transport, fertilizer production, and the production of inputs to poultry feeds 
2) Quantify supply chain water, land and energy use, along with greenhouse gas, acidifying, and eutrophying emissions associ- ated with egg production in Canada in 2012 compared to 1962 both per kg of eggs produced, and for industry-wide production in aggregate. 
3) Evaluate the resource and environmental performance gains associated with specific technological and husbandry advance- ments over the past 50 years, differentiating between changes attributable to supply chain versus farm-level activities.</list>

The study results are intended to support Canadian egg farmers and their supply chain partners in better understanding how changes in both supply chain activities and at the farm-level over time have influenced the resource use and environmental impacts associated with Canadian eggs as a basis for developing sustain- ability management strategies for the future. The study builds on an earlier benchmark study of the resource and environmental per- formance for the Canadian egg industry as a whole in 2012 (Pelletier, 2017), which is the source of the contemporary model of egg production, and methods for temporally-resolved modeling of agri-food supply chains reported by Pelletier et al. (2014).

The study results are intended to support Canadian egg farmers and their supply chain partners in better understanding how changes in both supply chain activities and at the farm-level over time have influenced the resource use and environmental impacts associated with Canadian eggs as a basis for developing sustain- ability management strategies for the future. The study builds on an earlier benchmark study of the resource and environmental per- formance for the Canadian egg industry as a whole in 2012 (Pelletier, 2017), which is the source of the contemporary model of egg production, and methods for temporally-resolved modeling of agri-food supply chains reported by Pelletier et al. (2014).
8: The system boundaries for this analysis (Fig. 1 - from Pelletier (2017)) encompass all relevant material and energy inputs and emissions attributable to processes in the cradle-to-farm gate supply chains of Canadian eggs in 1962 and 2012. Several different housing systems are used in contemporary Canadian egg produc- tion facilities. The current study focuses specifically on conven- tional cage egg production, which accounted for 92% of layer hens housed in Canada in 2012 (see Pelletier, 2017 for detailed methods and inventory data descriptions), compared to egg production in 1962.
9: The functional unit employed is one tonne of eggs at the farm gate in Canada in either 2012 or 1962. However, a variety of rele- vant reference flows are also used to express and discuss impacts at each supply chain stage.
10: Allocation between co-products was based on the gross chem- ical energy content of co-product streams. This strategy is consis- tent with the second tier of the ISO 14044 multi-functionality hierarchy, which privileges allocation based on physical relation- ships. For a detailed discussion of this rationale, see Pelletier and Tyedmers (2011) and Pelletier et al. (2015).
11: The production and maintenance of infrastructure (for example, machinery and buildings) were not considered. In high production volume contexts, their contributions to life cycle impacts (when amortized over life span throughput) are typically negligible (Ayer and Tyedmers, 2009). Antibiotics, cleaners, and enteric fermenta- tion for poultry were also excluded due to low data quality and minor relevance for overall impact assessment results. Due to the
Background data from LCI databases (modified for 1962 model)
Foreground models developed using directly collected data

very small amounts of irrigation water used for production of agricultural feed inputs (see description and references in Pelletier, 2017), irrigation water was not included in the inventory models. Finally, as a result of considerable uncertainty regarding soil organic carbon sequestration, longevity, and losses in annual cropping systems, potential CO2 emissions from agricultural land use change were not considered.
For the model of the Canadian egg supply chain in 1962, effi- ciency changes in key "background" supply chain activities were considered. Specifically, these included changes in:

<list>the energy efficiency of producing primary energy carriers 
provincial electricity grid mixes 
freight transportation 
ammonia synthesis 
inputs and yields for agricultural and livestock feed input production</list>

Other potential changes were not considered. For "foreground" supply chain activities, direct inputs to breeder flock facilities, hatcheries, and energy inputs to feed milling (all of which contribute a trivial share of supply chain impacts) were assumed to be similar between 1962 and 2012. Detailed de- scriptions of methods, models and all supporting data for the 2012 model are available in Pelletier (2017).
12: The production and maintenance of infrastructure (for example, machinery and buildings) were not considered. In high production volume contexts, their contributions to life cycle impacts (when amortized over life span throughput) are typically negligible (Ayer and Tyedmers, 2009). Detailed de- scriptions of methods, models and all supporting data for the 2012 model are available in Pelletier (2017).
13: Foreground system data are those that were directly collected from supply chain participants in the 2012 benchmark study (breeder flocks, hatcheries, pullet and layer facilities) (Pelletier, 2017), along with the parallel data collected from published liter- ature, statistical repositories, and personal communications for the 1962 egg supply chain model. In total, survey data used for the contemporary model directly represent roughly 10% of all laying hens nation-wide in 2012.
Background system data include the production and supply of feed inputs, water, energy carriers, and freight transport modes.

Background system data other than feed input production and processing models were derived from the EcoInvent database, but modified where possible to best approximate Canadian conditions by using Canadian energy inputs/mixes for both 1962 and 2012.
14: <header>2.2.1. Primary energy carrier and electricity models </header>

Energy is an important input to virtually all supply chain ac- tivities associated with the life cycles of goods and services. At the same time, the quantity and quality of energy inputs to otherwise similar activities may vary widely, depending on diverse factors including geography, technology and time. Differences in "energy mixes" (i.e. the kinds and relative proportions of energy carriers) that are used may result in quite different resource and emissions profiles for goods produced in different times and places - for example, where natural gas is used in place of coal to support a thermal process, or where hydro-electric power is used in place of fossil fuel-generated electricity.

For primary energy carriers such as coal, oil and gas, the same life cycle inventory models (from the EcoInvent database) that were employed for the 2012 egg supply chain model were adopted for the 1960's model. However, these models were adapted to reflect changing efficiencies in the energy industry over time. "Energy Return on Investment" (EROI) is a dimensionless ratio that reflects the energy efficiency of energy production - in other words, the amount of energy that is required to bring a unit of a given energy carrier to market. Declining EROI for fossil energy carriers is a well- documented phenomenon (Gangon et al., 2009; Freise, 2011; Guilford et al., 2011; Lambert et al., 2012; Hall et al., 2013). Sim- ply put, as easily accessible reserves are exhausted and increasingly marginal sources exploited (for example, fracking for shale gas or extracting tar sands oil) it is increasingly costly, from an energetic perspective, to bring fossil energy carriers to market.

Values for declines in EROI of major energy carriers were derived from literature (Table SI1). Based on these reported changes in EROI over time, scaling factors were applied to the contemporary energy carrier life cycle inventory models for the purpose of arriving at a 1960's proxy for the lower resource and emissions intensities of bringing primary energy carriers to market

Each Canadian province also has a characteristic electricity grid mix that has evolved over time. Moreover, egg production is not evenly distributed between provinces. An egg production volume- weighted national average electricity grid mix was hence devel- oped based on Canadian statistical data for egg production by province and Canadian provincial energy mixes for both 2012 and 1962. Breeder flock, hatchery and pullet production-specific elec- tricity models were similarly developed for 2012. For 1962, breeder, hatchery and pullet production activities were assumed to occur in the same province as egg production, hence a common electricity mix was employed. For the purpose of developing the supply chain stage-specific, production-weighted electricity mixes, 2012 pro- vincial grid mixes (Table SI2) were derived from Statistics Canada data. For the 1962 model, StatsCan data for 1977 were used for all provinces other than Ontario, Quebec, Manitoba and Nova Scotia, where 1962-specific data were obtained from the archived annual reports of provincial utilities and personal communications with utilities staff. It should be noted that potential differences in the distribution efficiency of Canadian electrical grids between the 1960's and present day were not accommodated as supporting data were not identified.

18: Freight transport models for the 2012 supply chain were derived from the EcoInvent database, but modified to employ "Canadian- ized" fuel input models. Natural Resources Canada (NRCAN, 2017) reports changes in the energy efficiency of Canadian freight transport modes dating from 2012 back to 1990 only. The estimated decline in the energy intensity of freight based on NRCAN data corresponds to an annual rate of decrease of 0.8% for road freight, 3.2% for marine freight and 4% for rail freight - all of which are substantially higher than observed annual decreases in the United States between 1970 and 2010 (0.4%, 2.2% and 2.2%, respectively, calculated in Pelletier et al., (2014) based on US Department of Energy data (USDOE, 2012). In light of the relatively short time interval for which Canadian data are available and the much higher estimated annual rate of energy efficiency improvements, it was decided to adopt the more conservative estimates derived from the US time series data. These estimates were used to scale the energy input components of the contemporary life cycle inventory models for freight transport in order to generate 1960's proxy inventories.

2.2.3. Fertilizer production models and national fertilizer mixes Life cycle inventory data models for individual fertilizer inputs were taken from the EcoInvent database. These models represent average production conditions in Europe, but were modified where possible to better approximate Canadian conditions. The International Fertilizer Industry Association (IFA, 2013) re- ports statistics for Canadian fertilizer consumption by nutrient and fertilizer type dating back to 1973. The 1973 Canadian fertilizer mix was adopted as the reference mix for fertilizer applied for feed input production in the historical layer supply chain model. Ammonia is currently produced in 11 plants in Canada, most of these in western Canada. The nitrogen fertilizer production model for 2012 assumed provincial production levels proportionate to the ammonia production capacity of 10 out of 11 Canadian ammonia plants as reported in NRCAN (2008) (Table SI3). Phosphate fertil- izers are assumed to be produced in Alberta by Agrium, which is the only phosphate producer in Canada. Agrium sources its concen- trated phosphate from the Martison deposit in Kapuskasing, Ontario. Potash is assumed to be produced in Saskatchewan, which is home to 8 out of 9 Canadian potash mines. The same provincial distribution of fertilizer production is assumed for the 1962 model. The production and use of nitrogen-based fertilizers, in

particular, contributes strongly to the supply chain resource and environmental impacts of agri-food production activities (Hasler et al., 2015). Energy requirements to produce nitrogen fertilizer are 4.5 times higher than that for phosphate fertilizer, and 5.7 times higher than potash (Gellings and Parmenter, 2004). According to IFA (2009), 87% of energy consumption in the fertilizer industry is attributable to ammonia production. However, the energy required per tonne of ammonia produced declined from 58 GJ in 1960 to 28 in 2010. On this basis, a scaling factor of 2.07 was applied to the energy inputs for the contemporary ammonia synthesis life cycle inventory in order to reflect 1960's conditions (Pelletier et al., 2014).

Kongshaug (1998) estimates net energy consumption for old (1970) versus average (1998) and best available (1998) technologies for the production of other fertilizer components. The productive use of steam that is generated as a co-product of sulfuric acid, nitric acid and phosphoric acid production distinguishes old from new technologies. These potential differences in Canadian fertilizer production technologies over time were not accommodated in the current analysis. They would likely make insignificant difference to overall results.

2.2.4. Modeling N, P and methane emissions from poultry manure Manure management strategies may entail liquid or solid manure handling and storage, variable storage times, and varied uses of the manure. In such cases, production-weighted average processes were defined based on the directly collected data in the 2012 benchmark study (Pelletier, 2017). These assumptions were similarly applied to the 1962 egg production model. All details regarding calculation of nitrogen and phosphorus excretion rates and methane emissions are as described in Pelletier (2017).

22: <header>2.2.5. Agricultural production models for feed inputs </header>

All data and methods for production of feed inputs for the 2012 model are described in Pelletier (2017). Life cycle inventory data tables for both 1962 and 2012 are also provided in the Supple- mentary Information file to this manuscript (Tables SI11-12).

StatsCan reports seeded area and production of field crops na- tionally dating back to 1870. These data were used to calculate five year average yields for Canadian crops sourced as inputs to egg supply chains for the years 1961-65. Annual yields for corn, soy and wheat in 1962 are calculated as 4.78, 1.93 and 1.38 tonnes/hectare, respectively.

Inputs to soy and corn production in Ontario (which produced almost 100% of Canadian corn in 1962) in 1975 are described by Swanton et al. (1996). Fertilizer and pesticide input data are adopted in proxy for inputs to Canadian production of these crops in 1962. Fertilizer and pesticide input data for wheat production in 1962 are based on US data as described in Pelletier et al. (2014).

Based on data reported by Smil et al. (1983), and the US Department of Agriculture (2004), direct energy inputs to US corn production declined 61% per unit production over the 45 year in- terval between 1959 and 2004. This is comparable to the estimated decline of 48% in direct energy inputs based on the data from Swanton et al. (1996) for 1975 and Jayasundara et al. (2014) for circa 2010 a 35 year time interval. Indeed, a linear extrapolation of this rate of decline for the direct energy inputs to Ontario corn pro- duction to a comparable 45 year interval results in an estimated 62% decline. This 62% estimation is therefore applied to the contemporary energy inputs reported by Jayasundara et al. (2014) in order to scale inputs accordingly for the 1962 model.

For direct energy inputs to soy production, a comparison of data reported by (S&T)2 (2010) for contemporary soy production compared to those reported by Swanton et al. (1996) for soy pro- duction in 1975 suggests a 52% decline over this interval. Extrap- olating to the mid 60's therefore suggests a 67% decline in direct
energy inputs. This estimated decline is applied to the direct energy input data employed in the life cycle inventory model for contemporary soy production for the 1962 model.

Historical data for inputs to Canadian wheat production were not located. Fertilizer and pesticide inputs for the 1962 model were instead based on data for US wheat production in 1964 (USDA, 1995). The estimated decline in direct energy inputs for Canadian corn production (i.e. 62%) was adopted in proxy for wheat in order to scale the energy inputs for contemporary wheat production for the 1962 model.

Methods for accounting for field-level ammonia, nitrous oxide, nitric oxide, nitrate, phosphate and carbon dioxide (from urea fer- tilizers) emissions are described in Pelletier (2017), along with as- sumptions regarding transportation of fertilizers, pesticides and seed.

24: <header>2.2.5. Agricultural production models for feed inputs </header>

All data and methods for production of feed inputs for the 2012 model are described in Pelletier (2017). Life cycle inventory data tables for both 1962 and 2012 are also provided in the Supple- mentary Information file to this manuscript (Tables SI11-12).

StatsCan reports seeded area and production of field crops na- tionally dating back to 1870. These data were used to calculate five year average yields for Canadian crops sourced as inputs to egg supply chains for the years 1961-65. Annual yields for corn, soy and wheat in 1962 are calculated as 4.78, 1.93 and 1.38 tonnes/hectare, respectively.

Inputs to soy and corn production in Ontario (which produced almost 100% of Canadian corn in 1962) in 1975 are described by Swanton et al. (1996). Fertilizer and pesticide input data are adopted in proxy for inputs to Canadian production of these crops in 1962. Fertilizer and pesticide input data for wheat production in 1962 are based on US data as described in Pelletier et al. (2014).

Based on data reported by Smil et al. (1983), and the US Department of Agriculture (2004), direct energy inputs to US corn production declined 61% per unit production over the 45 year in- terval between 1959 and 2004. This is comparable to the estimated decline of 48% in direct energy inputs based on the data from Swanton et al. (1996) for 1975 and Jayasundara et al. (2014) for circa 2010 a 35 year time interval. Indeed, a linear extrapolation of this rate of decline for the direct energy inputs to Ontario corn pro- duction to a comparable 45 year interval results in an estimated 62% decline. This 62% estimation is therefore applied to the contemporary energy inputs reported by Jayasundara et al. (2014) in order to scale inputs accordingly for the 1962 model.

For direct energy inputs to soy production, a comparison of data reported by (S&T)2 (2010) for contemporary soy production compared to those reported by Swanton et al. (1996) for soy pro- duction in 1975 suggests a 52% decline over this interval. Extrap- olating to the mid 60's therefore suggests a 67% decline in direct
energy inputs. This estimated decline is applied to the direct energy input data employed in the life cycle inventory model for contemporary soy production for the 1962 model.

Historical data for inputs to Canadian wheat production were not located. Fertilizer and pesticide inputs for the 1962 model were instead based on data for US wheat production in 1964 (USDA, 1995). The estimated decline in direct energy inputs for Canadian corn production (i.e. 62%) was adopted in proxy for wheat in order to scale the energy inputs for contemporary wheat production for the 1962 model.

Methods for accounting for field-level ammonia, nitrous oxide, nitric oxide, nitrate, phosphate and carbon dioxide (from urea fer- tilizers) emissions are described in Pelletier (2017), along with as- sumptions regarding transportation of fertilizers, pesticides and seed.
25: Processing of wheat, soy, corn and canola was based on in- ventory data reported by Pelletier et al. (2009, 2010 a,b; 2013; 2014). These data were applied to both the 2012 and 1962 models.
26: <header>2.2.7. Livestock production models for feed inputs </header>

A variety of materials derived from rendered livestock products were reported as inputs to feeds utilized in the layer supply chain. The life cycle inventory models for beef and swine production employed in the current study were adopted from studies by Pel- letier and colleagues of contemporary production systems in the mid-western United States (Pelletier et al., 2010a,b), with all US supply chain inputs replaced with equivalent Canadian inputs. Rendered poultry products were assumed to be derived from spent hens as modeled in the current analysis.

For ruminant production in 1962, the contemporary grass-fed beef production model reported by Pelletier et al. (2010a) was adopted in proxy. The low-performance niche pork production model reported by Pelletier et al. (2010b) was adopted to represent pork production conditions in the 1960's. Spent hens destined for rendering as modeled in the 1960's model were assumed to be used for the production of poultry by-product meal and fat. All sup- porting supply chain activities such as energy, fertilizer and crop production were modeled using the previously described 1960's life cycle inventory models for Canada.
27: <header>2.2.8. Livestock material feed input processing models </header>

Data for the production of ruminant, poultry and porcine meat and bone meal and fat in 2012 and 1962 follow Pelletier et al. (2009, 2010 a,b; 2014), which employ the same modeling protocols and assumptions as the current analysis.
28: Minor feed inputs such as minerals, salts, amino acids and other supplements are modeled as reported in Pelletier (2017). The same models are applied for 1962 and 2012, although time-sensitive input models are employed.
29: Separate, consumption-weighted average feed formulations were modeled for each step of the 2012 egg supply chain (Pelletier, 2017). Pullet feed composition data for 1962 are adopted from Pelletier et al. (2014), as taken from Winter and Funk (1960). Layer feed composition data for 1962 are taken from a study by Aitken et al. (1969) that reports a comparison of rations employed at the Canadian Random Sample Egg Production Test (CRSEPT) during the 1960's The study involved 31 strains of layers entered into the CRSEPT in 1965-1966 and 1966-1967.
Proximate composition data for feed inputs are derived from
www.feedipedia.org. These data are used to calculate nutrient assimilation and losses.

Feed milling energy input data were derived from recent LCA studies of the US egg industry (Pelletier et al., 2014). These data were collected from eleven reporting facilities in the United States in 2010 and represent a total production of 2,679,405 tonnes of pullet and layer feed. Energy inputs to feed milling are assumed to be the same between 1962 and 2012.
30: All details regarding the 2012 pullet and layer production models are found in Pelletier (2017). For the 1962 model, chick and pullet production is assumed to occur in the province where pullets are set. Eggs set per pullet placed are assumed to be same in 1962 as in 2012. Data for pullet age when moved to layer houses and mortality rates (% of initial placement) are the average for all of the 36 strains submitted to the 1960-1961 Canadian Random Sample Egg Production Tests in BC, Alberta, and Central Canada (USDA, 1961). These include submissions from all provinces other than Newfoundland. Pullet body mass is based on Winter and Funk (1960). Pullet feed consumption is based on Pelletier et al. (2014) for US pullet production in 1960. Water consumption is estimated at 1.75 times the mass of feed inputs. Manure mass is calculated on an as-removed basis, assuming a similar ratio of feed use to manure production as observed in the model of contemporary egg pro- duction. Energy inputs to pullet facilities, and manure, chick, and feed transport distances are also assumed to be similar to those in the contemporary model.

Data for layer mortality (% of initial placement), age of spent hens, spent hen body mass, feed inputs per mass of eggs produced, egg weight and rate of discards are similarly derived from CRSEPT data for all strains tested in 1960-1961 (USDA, 1961). Water con- sumption, manure mass, and manure, pullet and feed transport distance are assigned as per the 1960's pullet model. Energy inputs are assumed to be proportionate to the contemporary model per hen housed, hence are scaled accordingly to reflect differences in egg production per hen.
31: All details regarding the 2012 pullet and layer production models are found in Pelletier (2017). For the 1962 model, chick and pullet production is assumed to occur in the province where pullets are set. Eggs set per pullet placed are assumed to be same in 1962 as in 2012. Data for pullet age when moved to layer houses and mortality rates (% of initial placement) are the average for all of the 36 strains submitted to the 1960-1961 Canadian Random Sample Egg Production Tests in BC, Alberta, and Central Canada (USDA, 1961). These include submissions from all provinces other than Newfoundland. Pullet body mass is based on Winter and Funk (1960). Pullet feed consumption is based on Pelletier et al. (2014) for US pullet production in 1960. Water consumption is estimated at 1.75 times the mass of feed inputs. Manure mass is calculated on an as-removed basis, assuming a similar ratio of feed use to manure production as observed in the model of contemporary egg pro- duction. Energy inputs to pullet facilities, and manure, chick, and feed transport distances are also assumed to be similar to those in the contemporary model.

Data for layer mortality (% of initial placement), age of spent hens, spent hen body mass, feed inputs per mass of eggs produced, egg weight and rate of discards are similarly derived from CRSEPT data for all strains tested in 1960-1961 (USDA, 1961). Water con- sumption, manure mass, and manure, pullet and feed transport distance are assigned as per the 1960's pullet model. Energy inputs are assumed to be proportionate to the contemporary model per hen housed, hence are scaled accordingly to reflect differences in egg production per hen.
32: Four mid-point impact categories and two category indicators were employed at the impact assessment stage. Global warming (CO2-equivalency over a 100-year time horizon according to IPCC, 2006), acidification (SO2-equivalency), and eutrophication (PO4- equivalency) potentials were quantified using the CML Baseline 2000 method (Guinee et al., 2001). The Cumulative Energy Demand (CED) method (Frischknecht et al., 2003) was used to quantify life cycle energy use.

Direct water and land use were calculated as indicators only (i.e. different land uses or water sources were not weighted using characterization factors). Saltwater withdrawals and water used for electricity generation were not included in the inventory.
33: For the current analysis, the interpretation phase focuses in particular on:

<list>elucidating the key drivers of resource use and environmental impacts associated with the Canadian egg supply chain in 1962 compared to 2012 
evaluating and comparing resource use and environmental impacts of egg production in 1962 and 2012, per unit production and in aggregate</list>
34: Detailed data for all life cycle inventory models other than the 1962 and 2012 layer facility models (Table 1) are available in Sup- plementary Information File 1 (Tables SI1-17). Average farm-level life cycle inventory data per tonne of eggs produced are notably different for the two reference years considered. Estimated inputs and outputs are considerably lower for all data points (i.e. 19-76%) in 2012, other than for average egg mass, which was very slightly higher in 1962, and rate of lay, which was 46% higher in 2012. For example, average number of pullets required per tonne of eggs produced in 2012 was 43% lower than in 1962. Other notable dif- ferences included feed use, mass of spent hens, mass of manure and mortality rate (35%, 53%, 35% and 75% lower in 2012, respectively).
35: Additional, detailed figures and tables for life cycle impact assessment results are provided in Tables SI17-21 and Figs. SI1-7.
36: The life cycle environmental impacts of fossil energy carriers were appreciably higher in 2012 compared to 1962 for all energy carriers other than coal (where impacts were lower in all impact categories except energy use) (Fig. SI1). These changes are primarily attributable to the increased energy intensity of extracting fossil energy carriers at present compared to 50 years ago (i.e. lower

energy return on energy invested). The energy intensity of fossil fuel extraction has risen more precipitously for oil and gas products compared to coal over the past 50 years. The lower burdens of contemporary coal, despite modest declines in the energy effi- ciency of coal extraction, are due to the reduced transportation- related burdens of coal production. These actually outweigh extraction-related burdens for coal. Since freight transport effi- ciencies have increased over time (despite declining efficiencies for fuel production) and these represent a larger share of the production-related environmental footprint of coal, the impacts of coal production have declined overall.
37: Changes in the environmental footprint profiles of Canadian provincial electricity mixes between 1962 and 2012 are highly variable (Fig. SI2). For some provinces, impacts are decidedly lower at present whereas other provinces show higher impacts for contemporary grid mixes. These changes reflect two factors. First is the changing (lower) energy efficiency of producing the fossil en- ergy carriers that have, in turn, been used to produce electricity over time. Second are the changing proportions of electricity gen- eration technologies that are employed in each province. For example, British Columbia electricity generation changed from a grid mix almost completely powered by hydro-electric generation in 1962 (99.2%) to a mix having a higher proportion (8%) of other generation technologies, including natural gas and biomass- powered generation. Development of natural gas-powered elec- tricity generation increased the associated acidifying and eutro- phying emissions for BC electricity. Manitoba, in contrast,

42: The province-of-origin, production-weighted electricity mix for egg production in 2012 is lower for all impact categories other than energy use (59% higher). Footprint reductions range from 5% to 31%, depending on the impact category considered (Fig. SI3). These changes reflect differences both in provincial grid mixes as well as the proportions of eggs produced in each province in 1962 compared to 2012.
43: The environmental footprint of road freight in 2012 is quite similar to that of 1962, with the exception of considerably higher supply chain energy use as a result of the declining efficiency of fossil fuel production (Fig. SI4). Here, the modest fuel efficiency gains achieved for road freight transport over the 50 years considered (roughly 20%) are closely offset by the higher impacts of the fuels consumed. Due to more substantial improvements in the fuel efficiency of rail and marine freight transport over time, the contemporary footprints of these freight transport modes are, in contrast, generally lower now compared to 50 years ago. Supply chain acidifying, eutrophying and GHG emissions evince the most pronounced declines over this interval.
44: The environmental footprint of the average Canadian nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium fertilizer mixes have similarly improved in all categories other than supply chain energy use (Fig. SI5). These improvements are associated with changes in the proportions of different fertilizers that have contributed to Canadian agriculture over time, supply chain changes in efficiencies related to energy carrier provision and freight and, for nitrogen fertilizer, substantial improvements in the energy efficiency of ammonia synthesis. Im- provements are most pronounced with respect to acidifying, eutrophying and greenhouse gas emissions.
45: The environmental footprints of feed inputs that were employed in both 1962 and 2012 (for example corn, soy, and animal meals and fats) are generally lower in 2012 (Table SI18). Important contrib- uting factors here include a decline in inputs relative to yield for field crops, and improved efficiencies in animal husbandry over time. Also important are improved efficiencies of fertilizer pro- duction. For example, average impacts per tonne of crop produced are 43% lower for corn in 2012 compared to 1962. For wheat, in contrast, energy, water, and land resource use efficiencies are higher in 2012 but so, too, are emissions. This results from improved yields over time accompanied by (and, indeed, likely driven by) increased input levels.
46: As a result of both improved efficiencies in feed input supply chains as well as changes in the composition of feeds (in particular, lower levels of animal-based meals and fats), the life cycle envi- ronmental footprints of pullet and layer feeds are significantly smaller in 2012 compared to 1962 (Table SI19). Impacts are lower for all categories other than supply chain energy use, which is slightly higher for pullet feeds in 2012. The differences are most pronounced for layer feeds, which have, on average, only 38% of the impact levels of 1962 layer feeds. For pullets, contemporary feeds are, on average, 69% as impactful as 1962 feeds.
47: The relevance of specific inputs to the overall, supply chain environmental footprint of pullet production has changed between 1962 and 2012 (Fig. SI6). In particular, the importance of feed has declined due to improvements in feed input supply chain resource efficiencies, while the relevance of other inputs has increased proportionately. In 2012, manure management replaces feed inputs as the most important contributor to acidifying emissions, and also plays a substantially stronger role in supply chain eutrophying and greenhouse gas emissions. For energy use, direct energy inputs to pullet facilities are of greater importance in 2012 due to reduced efficiencies of fossil fuel production over time. Both water and energy use also make non-trivial contributions to supply chain water use in 2012, whereas in 1962 their contributions were pro- portionately much smaller.

Overall, the resource use and emissions levels characteristic of contemporary pullet production are considerably lower than those seen in 1962 (Table SI20, Fig. SI7). With the exception of supply chain energy use (89% that of 1962) and acidifying emissions (51%), impacts per pullet produced have been reduced by over 50% across the impact categories considered.
48: Similar to pullet production, feed inputs have become propor- tionately less important a determinant of resource and environ- mental impacts over time relative to other inputs to egg production (Fig. 2). However, feed does remain the dominant contributor in four of the six categories for the 2012 egg supply chain - green- house gas emissions, energy, land and water use. Manure man- agement replaces feed inputs as the largest contributor to acidifying and eutrophying emissions in 2012. After feed inputs and manure management, pullets sourced for egg production also make non-trivial contributions across all six impact categories in both 1962 and 2012. Direct energy inputs to egg production make very small contributions across impact categories, and direct water in- puts figure as a modest contributor to supply chain water use only.

Overall, the reductions in the environmental footprint of Cana- dian egg production in 2012 compared to 1962 are even more pronounced than the changes observed for pullet production. Be- sides supply chain energy use, where 2012 levels are 59% that of 1962, impacts of egg production in 2012 are 19%-39% of those attributable to egg production 50 years earlier (Table SI21, Fig. 3).
49: The observed changes in the environmental footprint of Cana- dian egg production between 1962 and 2012 are attributable to a combination of (1) efficiency changes in the supply chain activities that support egg production, (2) differences in the composition of feeds provided to pullets and layers over time, and (3) improve- ments in productivity and resource efficiency at the level of pullet and egg production (Table Moreover, the relative importance of these variables differs, depending on the category of impacts considered. For example, the observed improvements in terms of acidifying emissions and water use are fairly equally distributed between changes in supply chain, feed composition, and pullet/egg production variables. For greenhouse gas emissions, changes in feed composition over time had the greatest influence in reducing the GHG intensity of Canadian eggs, whereas supply chain changes (improved crop yields, in particular) were most important for observed reductions in land use. The sole outlier in terms of the direction of change, of course, is with respect to supply chain en- ergy use. If not for changes in feed composition and efficiencies at the level of pullet and egg production, contemporary egg produc- tion would be considerably more energy intensive at present

51: Current levels of productivity, resource efficiency and animal health in the Canadian egg industry are considerably superior to those achieved historically, and efficiencies have similarly improved for important supply chain activities such as ammonia synthesis, freight transport, and feed input production. The current analysis provides a quantitative understanding of the actual scale of these gains based on life cycle thinking and assessment.

Immediately apparent from this analysis is the considerable magnitude of the gains that have been achieved. Per unit of eggs produced in conventional housing systems in Canada in 2012 (which currently accounts for over 90% of production nationally), cradle-to-farm gate impacts were, on average, roughly one third of those estimated for egg production in Canada in 1962. Specifically, acidifying emissions declined by 61%, eutrophying emissions by

68%, and GHG emissions by 72%, while life cycle energy, land and water use decreased by 41%, 81% and 69% respectively. The scale of observed change is greater than what has been reported by most other authors, whether with respect to eggs specifically (Cederberg et al., 2009; Vergé et al., 2009) or for other livestock products (Capper et al., 2009; Capper, 2011; Boyd and Cady, 2012; Hamilton et al., 2013). This is largely because most of these studies did not account for efficiency changes in "upstream" supply chain activ- ities, which, depending on the impact category considered, accounted for 10-71% of estimated changes in the current study. Efficiencies associated with supply chain activities generally improved over time, with the exception of fossil fuel production, where energy-return-on-investment ratios have declined substantially.

Also worth noting are the changes in the absolute scale of impact levels for the industry as a whole. Canadian egg production volumes increased from 43.4 million dozen to 65.7 million dozen eggs per year between 1962 and 2012 - an increase of over 50%. Despite this increase in production volume, the egg industry's

overall environmental footprint actually decreased in all emissions and resource use domains considered in this analysis. Industry total supply chain emissions for egg production were 41%, 51% and 57% lower for acidifying, eutrophying, and greenhouse gas emissions, respectively. Supply chain energy, land and water use were 10%, 71% and 53% lower.

These observed changes are broadly consistent with those re- ported by Pelletier et al. (2014) for a similar analysis of the US in- dustry. Specific differences arising from the unique features of Canadian compared to US production conditions and performance levels in egg facilities and along egg supply chains both at present and historically are however, quite interesting. For example, the contemporary US industry is characterized by large scale facilities competing in a free market context compared to the more numerous, small-scale facilities subject to supply management in Canada. These differences may explain, in part, some of the minor observed differences in efficiency levels achieved in each country. Fifty years ago, the majority of US production had already transi- tioned to cage-based production, whereas the Canadian industry was still in transition, with a non-trivial fraction of birds in free run housing. Historical performance levels were hence also distinct. Similarly, there were different "original conditions" with respect to the efficiency of supply chain activities in each country, and somewhat different evolutionary paths. This is clearly quite important, for example, with respect to the mix of energy carriers used in each country (quality and quantity of energy used is often a key determinant of life cycle impacts), as well as differences in changes in EROI between countries for some fossil fuels.

Changes in the environmental footprint of Canadian eggs are attributable to a combination of factors. These include improved efficiencies in the supply chain activities that support egg produc- tion. For example, the almost 50% decrease in the energy intensity of ammonia synthesis for nitrogen fertilizer production - one of the most resource and emissions intensive aspects of modern agriculture - played a strong role. Also important were improved crops yields relative to input levels for feed inputs sourced for egg production, as well as higher fuel efficiencies in the freight trans- port sector. These improvements helped to partially offset the ris- ing supply chain burdens associated with on-going decline in the energy efficiency of producing most fossil fuels over time. Since energy inputs are important to virtually every supply chain stage in contemporary food systems, this underscores the desirability of transitioning toward renewable energy resources in order to safe- guard the sustainability gains that have been achieved in egg pro- duction, and food production more generally.

A second critical element of improving the supply chain envi- ronmental footprint of Canadian eggs has been changes in the composition of feeds sourced for both pullet and egg production. Indeed, as the most important contributor to four of the six impact categories considered in this analysis (and second in the remaining two), feeds play an absolutely central role in determining the life cycle burdens of the Canadian egg industry. Least-environmental cost feed sourcing should therefore be considered a priority leverage point for sustainability management in this industry. This requires engaging value chain partners - in particular feed milling companies and feed formulators - in developing feed-focused supply chain sustainability initiatives. Looking further upstream along the supply chain, there are surely also important opportu- nities associated with identifying and/or developing more sus- tainable raw material production systems - both in terms of agriculture and animal husbandry. Old and new technologies, alike, including perennial cropping systems, biological nitrogen fixation, precision agriculture, and production of cold blooded animals for feed inputs (for example, insects) in either terrestrial or marine systems may offer considerable promise in terms of dramatically
reducing the supply chain burdens of future egg production systems.

Clearly also of great importance to the evolution of the Canadian egg industry over the 50 year interval considered have been the significant improvements in resource efficiencies, animal health, and productivity at the level of pullet and egg production. Pro- duction per hen housed has improved by almost 50% and feed conversion efficiency by 35% over 50 years, while combined mor- tality rates for pullets and layers have declined 63%. These im- provements are grace of the concerted efforts of genetics programs, as well as improved understanding and implementation of pullet and layer house management strategies for optimal production efficiency.

Looking forward to the requirements and challenges the egg industry will likely face with respect to demonstrating continuous improvement in sustainability performance, a priority area for research will be "sustainability genetics." The layer of tomorrow must necessarily maintain the high resource efficiency gains that have been achieved while thriving in alternative housing systems that satisfy market demands with respect to animal welfare con- siderations and also utilizing sustainable feed resources. Maxi- mizing nutrient use efficiency, through careful feed formulation, feed management, and manure management will be similarly essential. Evolving management strategies will therefore also be necessary in order to ensure animal health outcomes in the context of changing housing system norms.

The relative contributions made by evolving supply chain effi- ciencies, changing feed composition, and improvements in pullet and egg production systems vary depending on the category of life cycle emissions or resource use considered. For example, changing feed composition has been the most important factor in reducing the supply chain carbon footprint of Canadian eggs over time, whereas gains in energy efficiency have been most influenced by improved resource use and productivity in pullet and egg produc- tion. Reduced water use in contemporary egg production, on the other hand, has been strongly driven by changes in supply chain activities.

This observation underscore that effective, on-going sustain- ability management for the Canadian egg industry will absolutely require life cycle thinking, life cycle assessment, and life cycle management strategies. Importantly, this will require constructive engagement with partners and stakeholders along the entire value chain in order to ensure effectiveness in the pursuit of improved sustainability outcomes. The lessons of the past 50 years are indicative of the scale of change that is possible. The current chal- lenge is to develop and implement a shared vision and strategy towards enabling the sustainable egg industry of the future.

This is largely because most of these studies did not account for efficiency changes in "upstream" supply chain activ- ities, which, depending on the impact category considered, accounted for 10-71% of estimated changes in the current study. Efficiencies associated with supply chain activities generally improved over time, with the exception of fossil fuel production, where energy-return-on-investment ratios have declined substantially.

Also worth noting are the changes in the absolute scale of impact levels for the industry as a whole. Canadian egg production volumes increased from 43.4 million dozen to 65.7 million dozen eggs per year between 1962 and 2012 - an increase of over 50%. Despite this increase in production volume, the egg industry's

overall environmental footprint actually decreased in all emissions and resource use domains considered in this analysis. Industry total supply chain emissions for egg production were 41%, 51% and 57% lower for acidifying, eutrophying, and greenhouse gas emissions, respectively. Supply chain energy, land and water use were 10%, 71% and 53% lower.

These observed changes are broadly consistent with those re- ported by Pelletier et al. (2014) for a similar analysis of the US in- dustry. Specific differences arising from the unique features of Canadian compared to US production conditions and performance levels in egg facilities and along egg supply chains both at present and historically are however, quite interesting. For example, the contemporary US industry is characterized by large scale facilities competing in a free market context compared to the more numerous, small-scale facilities subject to supply management in Canada. These differences may explain, in part, some of the minor observed differences in efficiency levels achieved in each country. Fifty years ago, the majority of US production had already transi- tioned to cage-based production, whereas the Canadian industry was still in transition, with a non-trivial fraction of birds in free run housing. Historical performance levels were hence also distinct. Similarly, there were different "original conditions" with respect to the efficiency of supply chain activities in each country, and somewhat different evolutionary paths. This is clearly quite important, for example, with respect to the mix of energy carriers used in each country (quality and quantity of energy used is often a key determinant of life cycle impacts), as well as differences in changes in EROI between countries for some fossil fuels.

Changes in the environmental footprint of Canadian eggs are attributable to a combination of factors. These include improved efficiencies in the supply chain activities that support egg produc- tion. For example, the almost 50% decrease in the energy intensity of ammonia synthesis for nitrogen fertilizer production - one of the most resource and emissions intensive aspects of modern agriculture - played a strong role. Also important were improved crops yields relative to input levels for feed inputs sourced for egg production, as well as higher fuel efficiencies in the freight trans- port sector. These improvements helped to partially offset the ris- ing supply chain burdens associated with on-going decline in the energy efficiency of producing most fossil fuels over time. Since energy inputs are important to virtually every supply chain stage in contemporary food systems, this underscores the desirability of transitioning toward renewable energy resources in order to safe- guard the sustainability gains that have been achieved in egg pro- duction, and food production more generally.

A second critical element of improving the supply chain envi- ronmental footprint of Canadian eggs has been changes in the composition of feeds sourced for both pullet and egg production. Indeed, as the most important contributor to four of the six impact categories considered in this analysis (and second in the remaining two), feeds play an absolutely central role in determining the life cycle burdens of the Canadian egg industry. Least-environmental cost feed sourcing should therefore be considered a priority leverage point for sustainability management in this industry. This requires engaging value chain partners - in particular feed milling companies and feed formulators - in developing feed-focused supply chain sustainability initiatives. Looking further upstream along the supply chain, there are surely also important opportu- nities associated with identifying and/or developing more sus- tainable raw material production systems - both in terms of agriculture and animal husbandry. Old and new technologies, alike, including perennial cropping systems, biological nitrogen fixation, precision agriculture, and production of cold blooded animals for feed inputs (for example, insects) in either terrestrial or marine systems may offer considerable promise in terms of dramatically
reducing the supply chain burdens of future egg production systems.

Clearly also of great importance to the evolution of the Canadian egg industry over the 50 year interval considered have been the significant improvements in resource efficiencies, animal health, and productivity at the level of pullet and egg production. Pro- duction per hen housed has improved by almost 50% and feed conversion efficiency by 35% over 50 years, while combined mor- tality rates for pullets and layers have declined 63%. These im- provements are grace of the concerted efforts of genetics programs, as well as improved understanding and implementation of pullet and layer house management strategies for optimal production efficiency.

Looking forward to the requirements and challenges the egg industry will likely face with respect to demonstrating continuous improvement in sustainability performance, a priority area for research will be "sustainability genetics." The layer of tomorrow must necessarily maintain the high resource efficiency gains that have been achieved while thriving in alternative housing systems that satisfy market demands with respect to animal welfare con- siderations and also utilizing sustainable feed resources. Maxi- mizing nutrient use efficiency, through careful feed formulation, feed management, and manure management will be similarly essential. Evolving management strategies will therefore also be necessary in order to ensure animal health outcomes in the context of changing housing system norms.

The relative contributions made by evolving supply chain effi- ciencies, changing feed composition, and improvements in pullet and egg production systems vary depending on the category of life cycle emissions or resource use considered. For example, changing feed composition has been the most important factor in reducing the supply chain carbon footprint of Canadian eggs over time, whereas gains in energy efficiency have been most influenced by improved resource use and productivity in pullet and egg produc- tion. Reduced water use in contemporary egg production, on the other hand, has been strongly driven by changes in supply chain activities.

This observation underscore that effective, on-going sustain- ability management for the Canadian egg industry will absolutely require life cycle thinking, life cycle assessment, and life cycle management strategies. Importantly, this will require constructive engagement with partners and stakeholders along the entire value chain in order to ensure effectiveness in the pursuit of improved sustainability outcomes. The lessons of the past 50 years are indicative of the scale of change that is possible. The current chal- lenge is to develop and implement a shared vision and strategy towards enabling the sustainable egg industry of the future.

Looking forward to the requirements and challenges the egg industry will likely face with respect to demonstrating continuous improvement in sustainability performance, a priority area for research will be "sustainability genetics." The layer of tomorrow must necessarily maintain the high resource efficiency gains that have been achieved while thriving in alternative housing systems that satisfy market demands with respect to animal welfare con- siderations and also utilizing sustainable feed resources. Maxi- mizing nutrient use efficiency, through careful feed formulation, feed management, and manure management will be similarly essential. Evolving management strategies will therefore also be necessary in order to ensure animal health outcomes in the context of changing housing system norms.

The relative contributions made by evolving supply chain effi- ciencies, changing feed composition, and improvements in pullet and egg production systems vary depending on the category of life cycle emissions or resource use considered. For example, changing feed composition has been the most important factor in reducing the supply chain carbon footprint of Canadian eggs over time, whereas gains in energy efficiency have been most influenced by improved resource use and productivity in pullet and egg produc- tion. Reduced water use in contemporary egg production, on the other hand, has been strongly driven by changes in supply chain activities.

This observation underscore that effective, on-going sustain- ability management for the Canadian egg industry will absolutely require life cycle thinking, life cycle assessment, and life cycle management strategies. Importantly, this will require constructive engagement with partners and stakeholders along the entire value chain in order to ensure effectiveness in the pursuit of improved sustainability outcomes. The lessons of the past 50 years are indicative of the scale of change that is possible. The current chal- lenge is to develop and implement a shared vision and strategy towards enabling the sustainable egg industry of the future.
57: Current levels of productivity, resource efficiency and animal health in the Canadian egg industry are considerably superior to those achieved historically, and efficiencies have similarly improved for important supply chain activities such as ammonia synthesis, freight transport, and feed input production. The current analysis provides a quantitative understanding of the actual scale of these gains based on life cycle thinking and assessment.

Immediately apparent from this analysis is the considerable magnitude of the gains that have been achieved. Per unit of eggs produced in conventional housing systems in Canada in 2012 (which currently accounts for over 90% of production nationally), cradle-to-farm gate impacts were, on average, roughly one third of those estimated for egg production in Canada in 1962. Specifically, acidifying emissions declined by 61%, eutrophying emissions by

68%, and GHG emissions by 72%, while life cycle energy, land and water use decreased by 41%, 81% and 69% respectively. The scale of observed change is greater than what has been reported by most other authors, whether with respect to eggs specifically (Cederberg et al., 2009; Vergé et al., 2009) or for other livestock products (Capper et al., 2009; Capper, 2011; Boyd and Cady, 2012; Hamilton et al., 2013). This is largely because most of these studies did not account for efficiency changes in "upstream" supply chain activ- ities, which, depending on the impact category considered, accounted for 10-71% of estimated changes in the current study. Efficiencies associated with supply chain activities generally improved over time, with the exception of fossil fuel production, where energy-return-on-investment ratios have declined substantially.

Also worth noting are the changes in the absolute scale of impact levels for the industry as a whole. Canadian egg production volumes increased from 43.4 million dozen to 65.7 million dozen eggs per year between 1962 and 2012 - an increase of over 50%. Despite this increase in production volume, the egg industry's

overall environmental footprint actually decreased in all emissions and resource use domains considered in this analysis. Industry total supply chain emissions for egg production were 41%, 51% and 57% lower for acidifying, eutrophying, and greenhouse gas emissions, respectively. Supply chain energy, land and water use were 10%, 71% and 53% lower.

These observed changes are broadly consistent with those re- ported by Pelletier et al. (2014) for a similar analysis of the US in- dustry. Specific differences arising from the unique features of Canadian compared to US production conditions and performance levels in egg facilities and along egg supply chains both at present and historically are however, quite interesting. For example, the contemporary US industry is characterized by large scale facilities competing in a free market context compared to the more numerous, small-scale facilities subject to supply management in Canada. These differences may explain, in part, some of the minor observed differences in efficiency levels achieved in each country. Fifty years ago, the majority of US production had already transi- tioned to cage-based production, whereas the Canadian industry was still in transition, with a non-trivial fraction of birds in free run housing. Historical performance levels were hence also distinct. Similarly, there were different "original conditions" with respect to the efficiency of supply chain activities in each country, and somewhat different evolutionary paths. This is clearly quite important, for example, with respect to the mix of energy carriers used in each country (quality and quantity of energy used is often a key determinant of life cycle impacts), as well as differences in changes in EROI between countries for some fossil fuels.

Changes in the environmental footprint of Canadian eggs are attributable to a combination of factors. These include improved efficiencies in the supply chain activities that support egg produc- tion. For example, the almost 50% decrease in the energy intensity of ammonia synthesis for nitrogen fertilizer production - one of the most resource and emissions intensive aspects of modern agriculture - played a strong role. Also important were improved crops yields relative to input levels for feed inputs sourced for egg production, as well as higher fuel efficiencies in the freight trans- port sector. These improvements helped to partially offset the ris- ing supply chain burdens associated with on-going decline in the energy efficiency of producing most fossil fuels over time. Since energy inputs are important to virtually every supply chain stage in contemporary food systems, this underscores the desirability of transitioning toward renewable energy resources in order to safe- guard the sustainability gains that have been achieved in egg pro- duction, and food production more generally.

A second critical element of improving the supply chain envi- ronmental footprint of Canadian eggs has been changes in the composition of feeds sourced for both pullet and egg production. Indeed, as the most important contributor to four of the six impact categories considered in this analysis (and second in the remaining two), feeds play an absolutely central role in determining the life cycle burdens of the Canadian egg industry. Least-environmental cost feed sourcing should therefore be considered a priority leverage point for sustainability management in this industry. This requires engaging value chain partners - in particular feed milling companies and feed formulators - in developing feed-focused supply chain sustainability initiatives. Looking further upstream along the supply chain, there are surely also important opportu- nities associated with identifying and/or developing more sus- tainable raw material production systems - both in terms of agriculture and animal husbandry. Old and new technologies, alike, including perennial cropping systems, biological nitrogen fixation, precision agriculture, and production of cold blooded animals for feed inputs (for example, insects) in either terrestrial or marine systems may offer considerable promise in terms of dramatically
reducing the supply chain burdens of future egg production systems.

Clearly also of great importance to the evolution of the Canadian egg industry over the 50 year interval considered have been the significant improvements in resource efficiencies, animal health, and productivity at the level of pullet and egg production. Pro- duction per hen housed has improved by almost 50% and feed conversion efficiency by 35% over 50 years, while combined mor- tality rates for pullets and layers have declined 63%. These im- provements are grace of the concerted efforts of genetics programs, as well as improved understanding and implementation of pullet and layer house management strategies for optimal production efficiency.

The layer of tomorrow must necessarily maintain the high resource efficiency gains that have been achieved while thriving in alternative housing systems that satisfy market demands with respect to animal welfare con- siderations and also utilizing sustainable feed resources. Maxi- mizing nutrient use efficiency, through careful feed formulation, feed management, and manure management will be similarly essential. Evolving management strategies will therefore also be necessary in order to ensure animal health outcomes in the context of changing housing system norms.

The relative contributions made by evolving supply chain effi- ciencies, changing feed composition, and improvements in pullet and egg production systems vary depending on the category of life cycle emissions or resource use considered. For example, changing feed composition has been the most important factor in reducing the supply chain carbon footprint of Canadian eggs over time, whereas gains in energy efficiency have been most influenced by improved resource use and productivity in pullet and egg produc- tion. Reduced water use in contemporary egg production, on the other hand, has been strongly driven by changes in supply chain activities.

This observation underscore that effective, on-going sustain- ability management for the Canadian egg industry will absolutely require life cycle thinking, life cycle assessment, and life cycle management strategies. Importantly, this will require constructive engagement with partners and stakeholders along the entire value chain in order to ensure effectiveness in the pursuit of improved sustainability outcomes. The lessons of the past 50 years are indicative of the scale of change that is possible. The current chal- lenge is to develop and implement a shared vision and strategy towards enabling the sustainable egg industry of the future.
